text: The laparoscopic ports are positioned as shown, with the surgeon standing on the patient's left side. The gallbladder is grasped at the fundus and traction is applied towards the patient's right shoulder, providing clear exposure of the Calot's triangle anatomy. The surgeon's left hand holds the infundibulum.

The surgeon moves the gallbladder in a leg-like manner, revealing the common bile duct (CBD) and gallbladder. A burst of energy is applied at the peritoneal fold of the gallbladder, clearing away fat and utilizing pneumoperitoneum for posterior dissection. The liver is visible, and anterior dissection is performed close to the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, cystic duct, and common bile duct within the Calot's triangle. Clips are applied to ligate the cystic artery, with traction on the gallbladder fundus released to prevent injury to the common bile duct.
Two clips are applied on the body side and one on the specimen side. The surgeon uses a ligature device to remove the thin-walled gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon demonstrates a bloodless procedure, with no visible RBCs in the surgical field. The cystic duct is grasped and its contents, including gallstones, are removed using an artery forceps.
